questions.text: How wide is 0.1 mrad at 200 m?

questions.text: A deer-sized feature is estimated at 200 mm and measures 1 mrad in the reticle. Approximately how far away is it?
questions.explanation: Distance equals target size in millimetres divided by mrad. 200 ÷ 1 = 200 m.

questions.text: A deer has an assumed 200 mm vital zone. Your rifle shoots 0.3 mrad and wind uncertainty is ±0.1 mrad at 300 m. Is this a high-confidence shot based on these factors?
questions.explanation: The assumed vital zone is 200 mm. Rifle dispersion is 0.3 × 300 = 90 mm. Wind uncertainty is 0.1 × 300 = 30 mm. Together this represents about 120 mm of possible error before considering shooter position, animal movement or range error.

questions.text: Your spotter calls the impact 0.2 mrad left at 300 m. What correction should you apply?
questions.explanation: Correct in the opposite direction of the miss. At 300 m, 0.2 mrad is 60 mm, but the scope/hold correction remains 0.2 mrad right.

questions.text: Your spotter calls the impact 0.3 mrad right at 400 m. What correction should you apply?
questions.explanation: Correct in the opposite direction of the miss. At 400 m, 0.3 mrad is 120 mm, but the scope/hold correction remains 0.3 mrad left.

questions.text: A deer is at 220 m. The required elevation correction is 0.3 mrad and the animal is stationary. Should you dial or hold?
questions.explanation: At 220 m the correction is 0.3 mrad. Dialing is usually better for larger, deliberate corrections when time allows. Holding is usually better for small corrections, fast opportunities, or animals likely to move.

questions.text: A deer is at 520 m. The required elevation correction is 1.1 mrad and the animal is standing briefly. Should you dial or hold?
questions.explanation: At 520 m the correction is 1.1 mrad. Dialing is usually better for larger, deliberate corrections when time allows. Holding is usually better for small corrections, fast opportunities, or animals likely to move.

questions.text: A deer is at 800 m. The required elevation correction is 2.5 mrad and the animal is stationary but wind is uncertain. Should you dial or hold?
questions.explanation: At 800 m the correction is 2.5 mrad. Dialing is usually better for larger, deliberate corrections when time allows. Holding is usually better for small corrections, fast opportunities, or animals likely to move.

questions.text: A deer has an assumed 200 mm vital zone at 300 m. Rifle accuracy is 0.3 mrad, wind uncertainty is ±0.1 mrad, and possible elevation/range error is ±0.1 mrad. Is a first-round vital-zone hit highly probable?
questions.explanation: The assumed vital zone is 200 mm. Rifle dispersion is 90 mm, wind uncertainty is 30 mm, and elevation/range uncertainty is 30 mm. Combined possible error is about 150 mm, before accounting for shooter position, animal movement or stress.
